result.markdown:
--- title: "Conway's Blobs" jupyter: brainvomit execute: echo: false warning: false --- ## BlobSim `blobsim` is a small world that runs on a grid. Each cell can hold one **blob** — a simple creature with an energy level. Step by step, every blob senses its neighbours, picks an action (move, eat, reproduce, or rest), and pays for it in energy. Blobs that run out of energy die; well-fed ones reproduce. The grid itself holds energy that blobs eat, and that may or may not replenish over time. It starts from Conway's *Game of Life* — cells on a grid that live or die by their neighbours — and asks what happens when each cell becomes an individual that has to *earn its living*: carry energy, spend it to act, and starve if it can't. The result sits somewhere between a cellular automaton and a tiny ecology. ## Design The grid wraps around at the edges (a blob walking off the right side reappears on the left). Every step runs the same fixed sequence: > observe → decide → resolve who-goes-where → charge energy → act → absorb from the > grid → update the environment → remove the dead → record The behaviour of a blob ("rules"), the behaviour of the environment (decaying vs. replenishing), and how movement conflicts are settled are each swappable without touching the rest. ## Universal laws - **Conservation of energy.** At any moment, the energy held by living blobs, plus the energy left in the grid, plus everything spent so far, equals what the world started with (plus anything the environment has since added). A running tally re-checks this equality every single step, so a bug that quietly leaks or invents energy cannot hide. - **Determinism.** The same starting seed reproduces the exact same run, step for step. - **One blob per cell.** No two blobs ever occupy the same square. - **Reproduction only moves energy.** A new blob is funded entirely out of its parent's energy; splitting never creates any. - **Death is permanent.** A blob whose energy falls to the death threshold is removed immediately; every surviving blob sits strictly above it. ## Example A finite grid with no replenishment can only end one way — the blobs eat through the available energy and die out: ![Two kinds of blob draw down a finite grid until none are left.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc1_decay_extinction.gif) One run's population and total energy over time: ::: {#cell-live-sim .cell execution_count=2} ::: {#live-sim .cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: The *population* line (left axis) counts living blobs; the *total energy* line (right axis) is the energy currently held by blobs and the grid together. The run opens with a boom — the grazers find food and multiply roughly tenfold in the first ~20 steps — which overshoots what the grid can feed. The crowd then draws energy down faster than it regenerates, so the energy line sags and the population crashes back. From there the two lines settle into damped, out-of-phase swings (a population peak drains energy → the shortage starves the next generation → energy recovers → repeat), converging toward a steady level — here ~125 blobs — that the regenerating grid can sustain indefinitely. The shape to take away: **overshoot → correction → convergence to a carrying capacity.** ## Benchmark runs/base cases Each base case below computes a target *analytically from the rules* (a conservation identity, an expected lifespan, a stable population) and then checks the simulation lands on it within tolerance. Passing them all is a statistical precondition: evidence the engine is predictable and internally consistent, so that later, non-base-case results can be read as findings rather than bugs. ### Predicted lifespan **The rule.** A lone blob lives in two phases: an *active* phase where it can still afford to move (paying a higher average cost per step) and a *resting* phase, below that affordability threshold, where it can only idle at the fixed metabolic cost until it starves. Adding the expected length of both phases gives a closed form, $$E[\tau] \;=\; \frac{E_0 - B}{E[c]} \;+\; \frac{B}{M} \;+\; (\text{boundary correction}),$$ with starting energy $E_0$, resting cost $M$, average active cost $E[c]$, and the affordability threshold $B$ below which moving is no longer possible. For the test parameters this predicts **≈ 186 steps**; the run observed 185.9. ### Guaranteed extinction on a finite grid **The rule.** If the environment adds no energy, then every step at least some energy is spent and never returned, so the world's total can only fall. With a finite starting amount $E_0$ and a smallest per-step metabolic cost $m$, every blob must die within $$T_\text{max} = \left\lceil E_0 / m \right\rceil$$ steps — and reproduction can't postpone it, because offspring are funded from parents (energy only moves, it isn't created). The animation above shows exactly this. ### Boom-bust cycle **The rule.** Give blobs a rich grid and they multiply fast — but the peak is capped by *space*, not energy: with one blob per cell, the population can't exceed the number of cells. Once it fills the grid and the food is drawn down, it collapses to extinction. ![A population explodes to fill the grid, then crashes.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc4_boom_bust.gif) ### Carrying capacity **The rule.** When the grid replenishes, the population settles where energy coming in balances energy spent. Equating the two gives a predicted stable size $$N^* \approx \frac{\text{rate}\times(\text{cells})}{\text{rate} + \text{avg cost per blob}} \approx 106.$$ Starting from very crowded and very sparse, both runs converge to roughly the same level — evidence it's a genuine attractor, not an artifact of where you start. ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![Sparse start — grows toward the steady level.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc5_sparse.gif) ![Crowded start — relaxes down to the same level.](/assets/images/blobsim/sc5_dense.gif) ::: ### A lone blob's energy **The rule.** A single blob on a fast-replenishing grid absorbs more each step than it spends, so its energy climbs quickly to the maximum it can hold and then stays pinned there — steady, with essentially no variation. ### Extinction threshold **The rule.** Whether a population survives hinges on how fast the grid replenishes. There is a **critical rate**: below it, each blob can't absorb enough to replace what it spends, so on average it leaves fewer than one surviving offspring and the line dies out; above it, survival becomes possible. The crossover is sharp — a phase transition. ::: {layout-ncol=2} ![Below the critical rate — the population dies out.](/assets/images/blobsim/c2_extinction.gif) ![Above the critical rate — the population survives.](/assets/images/blobsim/c2_survival.gif) ::: ### Grazers vs. walkers **The rule.** Put two strategies on one grid: undirected **random walkers** and **grazers** that move toward the richest neighbouring cell. With identical metabolism, the grazers absorb more per step, so they reproduce faster and eventually take over. Greater efficiency compounds into dominance. ![Energy-seeking grazers outcompete random walkers.](/assets/images/blobsim/c3_competition.gif)
